summary: A biographical essay detailing Dr. Virginia M.-Y. Lee's journey from facing cultural and gender-based obstacles to becoming a world-renowned neuroscientist. It highlights her early struggles with self-doubt and lack of mentorship, her breakthrough realization of her own capabilities, and her advice to younger female scientists about balancing family and a long career.

principles.statement: Careers are long, so it is okay to take the time you need for family life.
principles.rationale: Women often feel pressured to manage everything at once, but a scientific career can span decades, allowing ample time to balance both family and work over the long term.
principles.source_quote: Even if you take time off for family life, remember that careers are very long. Take the time you need, and know that you can work for a long time.

principles.statement: True scientific success is measured by the reproducibility and expanded application of your research by others.
principles.rationale: A discovery is most valuable when other scientists can independently verify it and build upon it to create new applications.
principles.source_quote: What was so fantastic is that people were able to repeat our research, and now are coming up with new applications and ways to use it.

principles.statement: Self-belief is a crucial prerequisite for sustaining a career in science.
principles.rationale: Especially for women who may lack early mentorship or face societal doubt, proving to yourself that you are capable is the foundational breakthrough needed to succeed.
principles.source_quote: I said to myself, I can. I can go to school full time. I can run my grants. Clearly, I can sustain myself as a scientist.

mental_models.name: The 'I Can' Mantra (Self-Efficacy Loop)
mental_models.description: Using tangible, difficult achievements as undeniable proof of one's own competence to overcome imposter syndrome and societal doubt.
mental_models.example: When she successfully received two prestigious grants during her first year of a demanding executive MBA program, she used that concrete success to prove to herself that she could sustain herself as a scientist.

heuristics: Your life is going to be very busy, but it’s all doable.

signature_quotes.text: I said to myself, I can. I can go to school full time. I can run my grants. Clearly, I can sustain myself as a scientist.
signature_quotes.context: Describing her personal breakthrough when she realized her own capabilities despite a lack of early mentorship.

signature_quotes.text: Your life is going to be very busy, but it’s all doable. Even if you take time off for family life, remember that careers are very long. Take the time you need, and know that you can work for a long time.
signature_quotes.context: Her advice to rising female scientists regarding work-life balance.

signature_quotes.text: What was so fantastic is that people were able to repeat our research, and now are coming up with new applications and ways to use it.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on her greatest accomplishment, the discovery of the TDP-43 protein.

signature_quotes.text: A large part of where I am today is that I’ve had a very long career. In two months, I’ll be 77, and I’m still working.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the compounding value of longevity in a scientific career.

anti_patterns.description: Believing that taking time off for family will ruin a scientific career.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Careers are very long, and there is ample time to return and sustain a long-term trajectory in science.

anti_patterns.description: Accepting cultural norms that dictate women should not pursue ambitious careers.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It prevents women from discovering how good they actually are and deprives the scientific community of their potential contributions.
